description: Romantic comedy requires two active co-equal leads in forced proximity, with every scene serving both comic and romantic engines simultaneously — the misalignment must be sustained long enough that overcoming it requires real effort.

character_constraints.description: An active co-equal lead with a distinct worldview that creates friction with the other lead.
An active co-equal lead — not a prize to be won but a full participant in the romantic negotiation.
A romantic competitor who raises the stakes but is not the true obstacle.
Friends, family, or colleagues who witness, comment on, and complicate the romance.

relationship_constraints.description: Both leads must share an unavoidable setting — workplace, social circle, neighboring spaces — that prevents retreat.
A competing romantic interest that clarifies what the leads actually want.

place_constraints.description: The workplace, apartment building, or social venue that keeps both leads in unavoidable contact.
A public or significant location for the climactic declaration — airport, rooftop, party.

object_constraints.description: An object associated with the initial meeting or misunderstanding — becomes a callback in resolution.
A gift, letter, or token that carries emotional weight in the climactic scene.

element_rules.description: Every scene must serve both the comic engine and the romantic engine simultaneously — pure comedy or pure romance scenes are structural failures.
element_rules.testable_condition: Each scene in the timeline contains both a comic complication and a romantic development.

element_rules.description: The misalignment between leads must be sustained long enough that overcoming it requires real effort — no easy or premature resolution.
element_rules.testable_condition: The romantic resolution occurs in the final quarter of the timeline, not before.
